Q1: I tried to create a consistent routine, but honestly it was hard with limited resources at home. I usually studied in blocks of about three hours when I could find quiet time, often late at night or early mornings. I'd write out weekly goals in a notebook and check things off as I went. With 21 hours of study time to manage across 17 online courses, I had to be really strategic about what got my attention each week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to keep some regularity, like reviewing materials on weekends, but deadlines would throw everything off. When multiple assignments piled up, I'd shift into survival mode and just focus on whatever was most urgent. My extracurriculars also meant some weeks were crazier than others.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an upcoming deadline or knowing I needed to check discussion posts for a specific module. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there was new content or announcements I might have missed. I made it a habit to check at least once a day, even if briefly, because with so many courses I couldn't afford to fall behind on important updates.
Q4: Yeah, after about the third week it became automatic for me to log in during my morning coffee. I set it up as part of my routine because I knew if I didn't, things would slip through the cracks. The mindfulness practice I started doing actually helped me be more intentional about these habits. It became less of a chore and more just something I did, like checking messages.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were my lifeline since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and annotate them heavily. Discussion posts were also valuable because I could see how other students interpreted concepts, which helped me check my own understanding. The structured modules that broke content into clear sections made it easier to tackle things piece by piece.
Q6: I often skipped the longer video lectures or only watched parts of them. With limited internet sometimes and my preference for reading, videos felt inefficient for me. I'd rather read a transcript if one was available. Some of the optional supplementary readings I also delayed because I had to prioritize what was actually being assessed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand a concept I was struggling with, I'd invest the effort. Also, anything that let me interact with peers felt valuable because it broke up the isolation of studying alone with limited resources. Practical applications always motivated me more than pure theory.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones got priority because they affected my grade directly. But sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting, so I'd save it for when I had breathing room. I noticed I engaged more deeply with optional content when I chose to do it, versus required work that sometimes felt like just checking boxes.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could and work from printed notes or saved files because my internet isn't always reliable. I also used textbooks from the library and would write extensive summaries by hand. A lot of my learning happened through rewriting and reorganizing information in my own words.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd study for hours from my printed notes and handwritten summaries without touching the LMS at all. I'd work through practice problems in notebooks, discuss concepts with a study group friend over the phone, or read chapters from borrowed textbooks. The LMS would show nothing for those sessions even though I was deeply engaged with the material.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a module's materials in one session, and then spend days working through them offline. My attendance percentage might look low because I wasn't constantly logging back in, but I was studying those same materials repeatedly. It was more efficient for me to batch my downloads and then work independently.
Q12: Around midterms when everything piled up at once, it got really hard. I also had weeks where family responsibilities at home demanded more of my time and energy. There were moments when I felt stretched thin trying to balance everything. But I've learned to push through those periods—I just had to accept that some things would be done imperfectly rather than not at all.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or family stuff, I'd log in but just do the bare minimum. Sometimes I was too mentally tired to engage with discussion posts even though I knew participating would help. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly—the LMS activities that could wait often did wait.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings and my mind would be elsewhere. I'd scroll through content without really absorbing it because I was preoccupied with other stresses. Those sessions felt like going through the motions. I learned to recognize when this was happening and would step away to do some mindfulness breathing before trying again.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts more than I should have because they felt less urgent than assignments with hard deadlines. Sometimes I'd avoid starting a difficult reading because I knew it would take mental energy I didn't have in that moment. I also delayed some assignments when I felt uncertain about the requirements, which probably wasn't the smartest strategy.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external structure. I'd map out all due dates at the start of each month and work backwards. My LMS activity would spike in the days before major deadlines as I finalized and submitted work. Without deadlines, I think I would have struggled more to pace myself across so many courses.
Q17: When I got feedback that was specific and constructive, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. Good feedback made me feel like the instructor actually saw my effort, which mattered a lot. But when feedback was delayed or generic, it was harder to stay motivated. I tried to use feedback to adjust my approach for the next assignment.
Q18: The modular structure helped me break down what felt overwhelming into manageable pieces. Clear weekly expectations made it easier to plan around my other commitments. But sometimes the pacing felt too fast given how many courses I was juggling. I appreciated when modules built on each other logically rather than jumping around.
Q19: Having materials available for download made everything easier given my internet situation. Clear rubrics helped me focus my limited time on what actually mattered for grades. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when important information was buried in long video lectures I had to hunt through. Consistency in formatting across modules would have helped too.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information, trying to understand requirements, or feeling lost and clicking around without knowing what to do. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. They might also be dealing with anxiety that makes them check things repeatedly without actually completing work. Or they could be engaged but struggling with the actual assignments.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My attendance looks low but I earned a Merit because I was studying extensively offline. Once I understood the material, I didn't need to keep revisiting the LMS. I think efficient learners can extract what they need quickly and then do the real work elsewhere. Low clicks don't mean low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students are active and which resources they click on. They can flag students who completely disappear. But they miss all the offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just clicking through, and the personal circumstances affecting someone's patterns. They show behavior but not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and apply what I'm learning, not just completing tasks. It's about curiosity and wanting to master the material, even when it's hard. For me, engagement also means connecting with peers and feeling like part of a learning community. It's mental presence, not just physical or digital presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from printed materials at my kitchen table, or the discussions I had with my study partner over the phone. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices that helped me stay focused and resilient. It can't see that I'm working with limited resources but still pushing myself to succeed. My determination and growth mindset are invisible to the data.
